moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, a flexible system that avoids the theoretical battles of 1.e4 and 1.d4. This move controls the d5 square and prepares to support our center with moves like e3 or g3 later. The beauty of 1.c4 is that we keep our options open—we can set up a kingside fianchetto, play e3 and d4, or even transpose into a Reti setup. You're aiming for long-term positional pressure rather than immediate tactical fireworks.

Black's most flexible response, developing naturally while keeping options for both ...e5 and ...e6 setups. In this Anglo-Lithuanian Variation specifically, Black accepts a symmetrical position while maintaining piece activity. This move contests the d4 square and prepares potential counterplay—Black isn't afraid to meet your pressure head-on. You should be prepared for moves like ...e5, ...Nf6, or even ...f5, as Black has room to maneuver in this relatively open position.
